# Robustness {#robustness-1}

This section reports three checks on the treatment-effect results. Each re-estimates the event study, matched controls, and synthetic controls, then sets the result beside the main estimates. The first holds the treated sample fixed across the three estimators. The second removes the product group that dominates the treated items. The third re-estimates on data from the Defense Logistics Agency alone, leaving out the supplementary BidLink transactions that extend coverage for many treated items but reach only a few of the controls.

## Common Treated Sample {#common-treated-sample}

In the Analysis section, the three estimators do not run on identical treated samples. Each imposes its own data requirements, and the synthetic-control estimator is the most demanding, dropping any item observed in fewer than three pre-waiver event years (so that there is enough pre-waiver history to build a credible counterfactual). The event study and matched controls do not impose that requirement, so they retain some items the synthetic-control estimator drops. The main estimates are therefore not, strictly, comparing the same items. This check runs all three estimators on the common most-restrictive sample of 22 items.[^17]  
On the common sample, the estimates stay close to their main-analysis values. For domestic sourcing, the event study holds at \-0.26 and synthetic controls at \-0.44 (against \-0.46), while matched controls give \-0.38 over the full post-period and \-0.23 at event year ≥ 2 (against \-0.40 and \-0.23). The long-term decline in price carries over as well: the matched-controls effect at event year ≥ 2 is \-0.17 (p \< 0.05) and the synthetic-control event-time path again turns significantly negative at event year 2 (-0.29, p \< 0.01), both close to the main analysis values, while the event study's post-period price path stays negative (average \-0.17). Offers show no effect in any estimator, same as in the main analysis. The results from the estimators thus survive using the uniform, more restrictive treatment sample.  
![][image24]  
![][image25]![][image26]![][image27]![][image28]![][image29]

## Excluding Freight Containers {#excluding-freight-containers}

Freight containers account for 21 of the 27 treated items, so there is a risk that the pooled estimates are being driven by a single product group. This check drops the containers and re-estimates on the items that remain. The remaining sample is small – at most six items, and as few as three for the price and offers outcomes under the synthetic-control estimator – so these estimates are imprecise, and one item can move a pooled figure.  
The immediate decline in domestic sourcing survives without the containers. In the waiver year the event-study coefficient is \-0.52 and the synthetic-control effect \-0.67, both sharply negative; matched controls, which pool the post-period into a single effect, give \-0.37, close to the full-sample value of \-0.40.  
In the long-term window, the trend in domestic sourcing turns positive. By the second post-waiver year the event-study and synthetic-control paths are near or above zero (event study \+0.20, synthetic controls \+0.12), though neither second-year estimate is statistically significant, and the matched-controls effect at event year ≥ 2 is \+0.36. Taken at face value, this shows domestic sourcing moving in the way the public waiver system intends – a disclosed supply gap drawing production back home.  
But the results are heavily flawed. The long-term aggregate is driven by one item: the shower bath fixture, whose domestic-sourcing effect at event year ≥ 2 is \+0.58 in synthetic controls and \+1.02 in matched controls. The other items observed that far out do not follow it: the materials-handling roller is still declining, and the ground-proximity warning system, sharply negative in the waiver year, recovers to roughly zero. The remaining three items drop out of the sample before the long-term window (Table A19). With one item pulling the average up and the next pushing it down, on a two-to-four item sample, the long-term increase does not indicate a market response. The Figure A13 shows the lone item set apart from an otherwise significantly negative group.  
Price and offers suffer from the same shallow sample. Mean offers shows no effect, as in the main analysis. The unit price estimate turns positive in all three estimators, but that is due to a single hearing-protection item whose price rose sharply after its waiver, set apart from the others in Figure A14.  
![][image30]  
*Figure A13. Synthetic controls, per-NSN treatment effect on domestic sourcing share (non-container treated items).*  
![][image31]  
*Figure A14. Synthetic controls, per-NSN treatment effect on maximum logged unit price (non-container treated items).*  
![][image32]![][image33]![][image34]![][image35]![][image36]![][image37]![][image38]

## Using Defense Logistics Agency Data Only {#using-defense-logistics-agency-data-only}

The transaction record for each item combines two sources: contract data from the Defense Logistics Agency, which covers every item in the analysis, and transactions recorded by BidLink, which extend that coverage for a subset of items. Many treated items draw on BidLink-recorded transactions, and for several of them, those transactions are what give the item enough activity before and after its waiver to measure at all. This check runs all three estimators on the DLA data alone, so the treated and control items are drawn from the same source.  
Using the DLA data alone leaves 23 treated items in the event study and matched controls, and 21 in synthetic controls. The items that depend on the supplementary transactions fall out, having too little DLA activity around their waivers to measure. The domestic-sourcing decline holds in all three, in both the immediate and the long-term window. The event-study path remains significantly negative across the post-period, averaging \-0.29 (versus \-0.26 in the main analysis). Matched controls give \-0.46 over the full post-period and \-0.31 at event year ≥ 2 (against \-0.40 and \-0.23), both still significant (p \< 0.05). The synthetic-control event-time path remains significantly negative at every post-waiver year (-0.44 at the waiver year, \-0.45 at event year 2), significant for 19 of the 21 fitted items.  
The long-term decline in price also holds: matched controls give \-0.20 at event year ≥ 2 and \-0.13 over the full post-period, and the synthetic-control event-time path again turns significantly negative at event year 2 (-0.29, p \< 0.01), while the event study's price path stays negative (-0.18). Mean offers again shows no effect except at event year \+3, which relies on only three treated NSNs.  
![][image39]![][image40]![][image41]![][image42]![][image43]![][image44]

[^1]:  "The steps that contracting officers must take to determine or document an exception will vary depending on the circumstances of the acquisition." (U.S. Government Accountability Office 2018).

[^2]:  "For example, a written determination from the Head of Contracting Activity (HCA) or a delegate may be necessary to determine non-availability in some cases. However, a written determination may not be required when an acquisition is conducted through full and open competition, is synopsized, and no domestic offer is received." (U.S. Government Accountability Office 2018\)

[^3]:  The Made in America Office would later be codified into law with the passage of the Infrastructure Investment and Jobs Act. (Congress.gov. "Text \- H.R.3684 \- 117th Congress (2021-2022): Infrastructure Investment and Jobs Act." November 15, 2021\. https://www.congress.gov/bill/117th-congress/house-bill/3684/text.)

[^4]:  Combined, the Department of Defense and Department of Veteran Affairs account for 2 to 3 percent of US medical-supply demand, according to congressional testimony from then-Executive Director for Procurement and Logistics at the Veterans Health Administration, Andrew Centineo. (Centineo testimony, VA Procurement: Made in America, September 20, 2023\)

[^5]:  DFARS 225.106(b): “Use an evaluation factor of 50 percent instead of the factors specified in FAR 25.106(b)(1)(i) and (c)(1)(i).” The factor is applied to the low foreign offer per DFARS 225.502(c). (“Subpart 225.1 \- BUY AMERICAN—SUPPLIES | Acquisition.GOV,” n.d.)

[^6]:  This is notably more than the 20% to 30% price preference offered by civilian agencies (“Buy American Act,” n.d.)).

[^7]:  Like the establishment of the Made in America Office, this requirement for written determinations was then codified into law by the Infrastructure Investment and Jobs Act. (Congress.gov. "Text \- H.R.3684 \- 117th Congress (2021-2022): Infrastructure Investment and Jobs Act." November 15, 2021\. https://www.congress.gov/bill/117th-congress/house-bill/3684/text.)

[^8]:  Code used for this thesis is available at https://github.com/Anantesh-Mohapatra/Waived-in-America.

[^9]: Transactions coded with place of manufacture code "C" (items not manufactured as end products, such as services coded under a manufacturing PSC) are excluded from the procurement extract. These contracts are not subject to the Buy American Act.

[^10]: Some waiver records cover multiple NSNs while some NSNs appear in multiple waiver records filed at different times. For example: there is a freight container waiver that lists 20 container NSNs.

[^11]:  Many code-A (full and open competition) awards are reported in FPDS without a populated number\_of\_offers\_received field. About 30% of (NSN, FY, event year) cells in the non-treated universe have missing mean offers fields, versus 1.7% of treated cells.

[^12]:  One of the 14 treated NSNs is dropped, as all three of its matched donors have blank offer counts on their event-year ≥ 2 transactions.

[^13]:  The standardized mean difference compares the treated and donor means relative to their pooled standard deviation.

[^14]: U.S. General Services Administration, Digital Analytics Program (U.S. General Services Administration, n.d.), 30-day window 2026-04-23 through 2026-05-23: madeinamerica.gov 4,141 visits versus sam.gov 6,462,354 visits.

[^15]:  50% for the DLA, 20-30% for civilian agencies ("Subpart 225.1 \- BUY AMERICAN—SUPPLIES | Acquisition.GOV," n.d.; "Buy American Act," n.d.)

[^16]:  The list has since been substantially shortened: a rule proposed in October 2024 and finalized June 2025 removed nearly two-thirds of its 109 articles. OMB has projected this would be the shortest the nonavailability list has ever been. (Fed. Regist. 2025; Office of Management and Budget, Made in America Office 2025\)

[^17]: One waived item, an enhanced ground proximity warning system, has no recorded unit prices. Matched controls draw their matches from each item's pre-waiver price, sourcing, and volume, so they cannot match this product and exclude it from every outcome. To keep one sample common to all three estimators, it is dropped throughout this check.
